topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom was thick with expensive perfume and forced laughter. Every glance I stole in his direction felt like a physical blow, a sharp, unwelcome jolt to my chest. He was laughing now, his arm around the bride, and the easy comfort radiating off him was a spotlight I couldn't escape. My hands were clammy, gripping a lukewarm glass of champagne so tightly my knuckles were white. I watched him from across the room, the meticulously arranged floral centerpiece between us seeming to mock my internal unraveling. When our eyes finally met—a fleeting, agonizing collision—the smile I had practiced for the last hour evaporated. I mumbled something incoherent to the waiter, my throat suddenly tight, and fled toward the balcony, needing the cold night air to scour the heat from my skin.

[story 2]

I knew I shouldn't have come. The invitation had been a flimsy excuse to drag myself into this gilded cage, but the pull of the familiar—of seeing him, even from this distance—was too strong to resist. He was standing near the punch bowl, looking effortlessly composed, a picture of everything I wasn't anymore. My breath hitched, a shallow, ragged thing in my lungs. Every nerve ending seemed hyper-aware, vibrating with a frantic energy that made the silk of my dress feel abrasive against my skin. I rehearsed a casual greeting in my head—something breezy, something that suggested nothing had changed—but the words caught in my throat, turning to ash. I forced myself to move, taking a shaky step toward him, my gaze locked on his profile, wishing the floor would simply open up and swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The music swelled, a saccharine melody that grated on my nerves. I found myself lingering near the edge of the crowd, pretending to examine a painting, anything to avoid the main thoroughfare where he stood. He caught my peripheral vision, and for a split second, the world narrowed down to just the space between us. A sudden, intense pressure built behind my eyes, a familiar, unwelcome tightening in my core. I felt a profound need to either run forward and shatter the carefully constructed peace, or simply dissolve into the shadows. My feet felt rooted to the marble floor, unable to shift, as I watched him turn slightly, and the briefest flicker of recognition sent a wave of nausea through me. I lowered my head, focusing intently on the pattern of the carpet, trying to will the sensation of his presence away.
